reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong comparison table leads the page and surfaces all the key differentiators (caching, speed, cost model, scope) in one scannable view — exactly what this audience needs to make a decision without reading the full page.

Pricing is directly grounded in the console source: ~$3/month EBS, per-instance-type compute rates, and ~$0.02/min CodeBuild are all confirmed in RunnerConfigModal.tsx and DeployNewProjectFromGit.tsx tooltips.
The per-project (EC2) vs. global (CodeBuild) scope distinction is clearly stated and correctly sourced from the RunnerConfigModal's inline note.
The 'Build runners and CI/CD' section with the deployment-method table is genuinely useful for developers who are unsure whether their CLI usage goes through the Console runner at all.
FAQ mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS-cost, and comparison questions well, and most answers are concise enough to function as Featured Snippets.
No mandatory style violations: no YAML Stacktape config, no StacktapeConfig type pattern, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, no marketing language, no multiple-command bash blocks.
problems: The entire 'CodeBuild configuration' subsection — compute types table (BUILD_GENERAL1_SMALL through BUILD_GENERAL1_2XLARGE), base Docker image override, and additional install commands — is not visible in either provided source file. The console source only exposes runner type selection and EC2 instance type; no CodeBuild-specific settings UI is present. Describing these as available options may lead users to look for settings that don't exist.

The 'AMI updates' subsection claims Stacktape 'automatically keeps EC2 runner AMIs up to date' and describes a specific reprovisioning workflow (terminate idle runners, reprovision on next deployment, never interrupt running builds). Neither source file mentions AMI management.

Specific EC2 runner runtime versions (Node.js 18/20/22/24 via fnm, Java Corretto 11/17/21, Ruby 3.3/3.4 via rbenv, PHP 8.1/8.2/8.3, Maven, Gradle, Composer) are not in any provided source. The source only lists runtimes generically. Citing specific version numbers that are wrong or outdated breaks user trust.

The final FAQ entry on runner failure describes specific operational thresholds: '30 minutes in a transitional state triggers reset' and 'stalled for 2+ hours marks deployment as failed'. These timeouts are completely absent from the source and appear invented.
The FAQ mentions 'Organization-level additional build commands' for EC2 runners. This is not confirmed in the source. It may be a CodeBuild-only concept that the writer has incorrectly generalized.

Minor: The 'Changing the instance type' subsection under EC2 runner configuration is nearly content-free — it repeats that the change takes effect on the next deployment, which was already stated in the intro paragraph and in the Switching section. It reads as a padded heading.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or rewrite the CodeBuild configuration subsections (compute types table, base Docker image, additional install commands). The provided source shows no UI for these settings in the Stacktape Console — the modal only exposes runner type and EC2 instance type. Either confirm this feature exists via a source file not provided, or collapse the section to 'CodeBuild settings (compute type, Docker image, install commands) are configured globally from the Projects page in the Console' without inventorying options the source does not show.
Remove the AMI updates subsection entirely. No provided source mentions AMI lifecycle management. If this behavior exists, it needs a grounded source file before it can be documented.

Remove all specific runtime version numbers from the EC2 runner pre-installed runtimes FAQ answer and from the comparison table row. Replace with the generic list confirmed by source (Node.js, Bun, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, Docker) without specific versions or package manager tooling details that are not sourced.

Rewrite or remove the runner-failure FAQ entry. The specific thresholds (30-minute transitional timeout, 2-hour stall detection) are not present in any source file and read as fabricated operational detail. If failure recovery behavior is worth documenting, it must be grounded in source.
optionalImprovements: Collapse the 'Changing the instance type' subsection — it adds no content not already in the parent section's intro or the Switching section. Remove the heading and absorb the one-sentence note into the EC2 runner configuration section intro.

The cost comparison math in the 'How does CodeBuild pricing compare to EC2 runner pricing?' FAQ uses $0.02/min for CodeBuild but the page's comparison table earlier lists no per-minute cost for CodeBuild in the 'Cost model' row — adding it to the table would make the table self-sufficient without requiring the reader to find the FAQ.

The DeployNewProjectFromGit source quotes ~$0.005/min compute for EC2, while the instance options table shows $0.006/min for c7a.2xlarge. The page consistently uses $0.006 (from the options table, which is more specific). A brief note that cost varies by instance type would clarify why the two numbers differ for readers who notice the tooltip vs. table mismatch.

Consider moving the 'EC2 runners vs CodeBuild' comparison table above the H2 intro paragraph — the audience wants to see the comparison immediately after the one-paragraph intro, not after another prose paragraph that partially repeats the table's content.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.

Hibernation behavior (15-minute idle threshold, ~10-second wake-up) is correctly extracted from both source files' tooltip text.
The per-project (EC2) vs global (CodeBuild) configuration scope distinction is accurately drawn from RunnerConfigModal.tsx which links to `/projects?buildRunnerSettings=open` for CodeBuild settings.
The EC2_RUNNER default for new projects is correctly identified from DeployNewProjectFromGit.tsx default state.
The 'Build runners and CI/CD' comparison table clearly separates Console-triggered deployments from CLI-triggered ones — useful grounding for engineers who mix both paths.
The opening section immediately answers the decision question; no filler or meta-framing.
No forbidden patterns: no YAML Stacktape config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, no PropertiesTable, no multiple ApiReference blocks.
problems: The FAQ answer for 'What happens if my EC2 runner fails or gets stuck?' cites specific timeouts (30 minutes transitional, 2 hours stalled) that do not appear anywhere in the provided source files. A production engineer relying on these numbers could be badly misled — these read as invented.

The pre-installed runtimes list in 'When to use EC2 runners' and the FAQ entry ('Node.js (18, 20, 22, 24 via fnm), Ruby (3.3, 3.4 via rbenv), PHP (8.1, 8.2, 8.3)...') is not present in any provided source file. The source only says 'Node.js, Bun, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, Docker' without version specifics. Specific version claims are unsupported and will drift.

The 'AMI updates' subsection describes a specific policy ('idle runners are terminated and reprovisioned... Running runners are not interrupted') that does not appear in any provided source. This is an invented operational claim about failure/update behavior, exactly what a production engineer needs to be accurate.

The CodeBuild 'Compute types' table (BUILD_GENERAL1_SMALL through BUILD_GENERAL1_2XLARGE with specific vCPU/RAM figures) is not in the provided source files. These are well-known AWS facts but are presented as Stacktape-grounded content — they should be attributed to AWS documentation or clearly framed as AWS CodeBuild limits, not implied to be Stacktape-controlled.

FAQ entry 'Can I customize the EC2 runner's build environment?' mentions 'Organization-level additional build commands' for EC2 runners, but the source only shows per-project EC2 instance type selection. The 'additional install commands' feature is described under CodeBuild configuration, not EC2. This claim is unsupported for EC2.

Minor cost inconsistency: the FAQ example uses $0.006/min for c7a.2xlarge (matching RunnerConfigModal.tsx) but DeployNewProjectFromGit.tsx tooltip says '~$0.005/min compute'. Not a blocker, but the page should acknowledge this is approximate.
mandatoryFixes: Remove the specific monitoring timeout numbers from the FAQ answer about runner failures ('30 minutes transitional', '2 hours stalled'). These are not in any provided source. Replace with: 'Contact Stacktape support if a runner appears stuck, or reprovision it from the runner configuration modal.' Do not fabricate behavior that production engineers will depend on.

Replace the specific runtime version list (Node.js 18/20/22/24, Ruby 3.3/3.4 via rbenv, PHP 8.1-8.3, etc.) with the source-confirmed non-versioned list: 'Node.js, Bun, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, Docker, and standard build tools'. Remove the fnm/rbenv version manager claims which are not in the source.

Remove or rewrite the 'AMI updates' subsection entirely. The operational policy described (how and when idle vs. running instances are updated) is not grounded in any provided source file. If AMI management is worth mentioning, write only what is confirmable: 'Stacktape manages the EC2 runner AMI. When a new image is available, it is applied between deployments.' Do not describe interruption behavior or timing that isn't sourced.

Reframe the CodeBuild compute types table as AWS CodeBuild service limits ('AWS CodeBuild offers the following compute types') rather than implying Stacktape controls them. Better still: omit the table entirely if it isn't configurable through Stacktape, or verify it against an AWS source reference. The provided source files do not enumerate these values.

Remove 'Organization-level additional build commands' from the EC2 customization FAQ answer. The source only shows per-project instance type selection for EC2. If additional build commands are only a CodeBuild feature, say so explicitly. Do not imply EC2 runners support a parallel 'additional build commands' mechanism without source.
optionalImprovements: Acknowledge the $0.005/min vs $0.006/min cost figure difference between the new-project flow and the modal — or use '~$0.005–$0.006/min' to bracket the approximation honestly.

The RunnerConfigModal.tsx falls back to CODEBUILD for projects where deploymentRunnerType is null (legacy projects), while new projects default to EC2_RUNNER. A sentence noting that older projects created before EC2 runners existed may default to CodeBuild would help engineers auditing existing projects.

The 'Switching runner types' section says 'the EC2 instance is eventually terminated and the EBS volume released after the idle timeout passes' when switching away from EC2. This is plausible but not confirmed by the source — soften to 'the EC2 instance is deprovisioned after the runner is no longer needed' if this is not source-verifiable.

Consider adding a brief note that the runner region is chosen at project creation and determines where the EC2 instance lives — the source tooltip in DeployNewProjectFromGit.tsx confirms 'Runner is per project and region, but you can change the default region later if needed', which is currently buried.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page opens with a clear answer-first paragraph and keeps the main decision framed around EC2 runners versus CodeBuild.
The comparison table is useful and cites concrete sourced values for runner type, caching behavior, hibernation, wake-up time, pricing model, and instance sizes.
The page distinguishes Console/GitOps runners from local CLI and custom CI/CD, which is important for the reader’s mental model.
problems: Several technical claims are not grounded in the provided source files: runners cloning the repository, installing dependencies, running the Stacktape CLI, dependency-install steps being skipped entirely, specific 4-5 minute versus 1-2 minute deployment times, AMI update behavior, runner replacement behavior, EC2 termination/EBS release timing, stuck-runner recovery thresholds, and deployments stalled for 2 hours being marked failed.

The page describes Console UI flows that are more specific than the provided source supports, such as changing settings from a project runner configuration modal, creating a project with a chosen instance size, and switching runner types from project settings. The source shows a modal and links, but not all described workflows.

The CodeBuild configuration section is weakly grounded. The provided source only says CodeBuild settings are configured globally on the projects page and that CodeBuild uses a fresh container with build-minute pricing; it does not show compute type options, base image configuration, or additional install commands.

The EC2 customization FAQ claims organization-level additional build commands for EC2 runners, but the provided source only mentions additional install commands in the draft and does not ground them for EC2 runners.

The FAQ includes useful questions, but several answers add unsupported calculations or operational behavior. The cost arithmetic and break-even guidance may be reasonable, but it goes beyond the exact source facts and should be marked as illustrative only or removed if strict source grounding is required.

The page has good comparison structure, but it overstates recommendations with unsupported thresholds such as “less than once a week” and “pays for itself after the first few deploys.” The source supports “recommended” and “infrequent deploys,” not those exact cutoffs.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or source-ground every unsupported operational claim: repository clone/install/CLI execution, skipped installs, exact deployment duration examples, AMI refresh behavior, runner replacement behavior, EC2 cleanup timing, stuck-runner reset thresholds, and 2-hour deployment failure behavior.

Rewrite the CodeBuild configuration section to include only facts present in the provided source, or add the source files that define CodeBuild compute types, base Docker image, and install-command settings.

Narrow Console workflow wording to what the provided UI source shows: the runner modal lets users choose runner type, save changes for the next deployment, and select an EC2 instance type only when EC2 runner is selected; CodeBuild settings are linked from the projects page.
Remove or qualify unsupported EC2 runner customization claims, especially organization-level additional build commands and deeper AMI customization guidance.

Replace unsupported precise frequency recommendations with source-grounded wording such as “EC2 runner is recommended for most new projects and active deploy workflows; CodeBuild fits infrequent deployments where avoiding idle compute matters.”
optionalImprovements: Add a short “What is sourced here” level of precision in prose by separating Console-supported facts from opinionated recommendations.

Keep the useful cost comparison, but avoid derived monthly totals unless the source set explicitly provides enough billing assumptions; otherwise present only the sourced per-minute and storage figures.
Consider shortening the FAQ cost answer to prevent it from becoming a second pricing model that may drift or exceed source grounding.
Use “approximately” consistently for all sourced prices and speed claims, including 2-5x and wake-up time.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the supplied Console runner sources on the main EC2-vs-CodeBuild distinction, default new-project runner selection, EC2 instance options, hibernation timing, wake-up timing, and pricing cues. The main problems are unsupported operational details that go beyond the two provided source files, especially lifecycle behavior, AMI maintenance, failure recovery, and CLI/CI interactions.

issues.statement: “Stacktape automatically keeps EC2 runner AMIs up to date. When a new AMI version is available ... idle runners are terminated and reprovisioned with the latest image on their next deployment.”
issues.reason: The provided source files mention EC2 runners, hibernation, caching, instance selection, and costs, but they do not describe AMI update behavior, replacement timing, or interruption guarantees. This is a Stacktape-specific lifecycle claim that readers could rely on operationally.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the AMI updates section, or narrow it to only the sourced fact that EC2 runners use a dedicated EC2 instance in the user's AWS account. Do not claim automatic AMI replacement unless a runner backend source is provided.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and includes the core choice: EC2 runners vs CodeBuild runners.
The comparison table is useful and concrete, with sourced costs, caching behavior, hibernation behavior, and runner-type tradeoffs.

The `m6a` versus `c7a` workload guidance is mostly AWS instance-family inference, not grounded in the provided Stacktape source. The source only provides the allowed instance types, vCPU, memory, and approximate per-minute costs.

The `Runner region` section says the runner region can be changed later. The source says the default region can be changed later in a tooltip during project creation, but does not show a runner-region update flow in the runner settings modal.
